sources.source: League of American Bicyclists / Uniform Vehicle Code
National Center for Missing & Exploited Children
REMS TA Center (US Dept. of Education)
USCG / National Center for Cold Water Safety

facts.fact: The pilot in command is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of the aircraft, and may deviate from rules in an in-flight emergency to the extent required to meet it.
Set the transponder to 7700 for a general emergency, 7600 for lost communications, and 7500 for unlawful interference (hijack).
Manage emergencies in priority order: Aviate, Navigate, Communicate.
On engine failure, immediately establish best glide speed and select a suitable landing area before troubleshooting.
Spatial disorientation is a leading cause of fatal general-aviation accidents when VFR pilots enter instrument conditions; trust the instruments, not bodily sensations.
Apply carburetor heat at the first sign of carburetor icing (unexplained RPM/MP loss in susceptible conditions).
Use 'MAYDAY' (spoken three times) for distress and 'PAN-PAN' for urgency on the radio when declaring an emergency.
Maintain at least a 3-second following distance, and increase it in rain, fog, or at night.
Seat belts reduce the risk of fatal injury to front-seat car occupants by about 45% (and by about 60% in light trucks).
In a skid, ease off the accelerator and look and steer in the direction you want the vehicle to go; do not slam the brakes.
During a tire blowout, hold the wheel firmly, keep the vehicle straight, ease off the accelerator, and slow gradually — do not brake hard.
If hydroplaning, ease off the accelerator, keep the wheel steady, and avoid hard braking until the tires regain contact.
Sending or reading a text at 55 mph takes your eyes off the road long enough to cover the length of a football field.
Move Over laws require drivers to slow down and, when safe, change lanes away from stopped emergency and service vehicles.
Never stop on railroad tracks. If your vehicle stalls on a crossing, get everyone out and away immediately, then call the emergency number posted at the crossing or 911.
If your brakes fail, pump the pedal to try to build pressure, downshift to a lower gear, and apply the parking brake gradually while steering to a safe runoff.
For a vehicle engine fire, pull over, turn off the engine, get everyone out and well away, and call 911 — do not open the hood into the flames.
Never drive into floodwater of unknown depth; turn around — water can hide washed-out road and sweep a vehicle away.
Use the SEE strategy — Search, Evaluate, Execute — to manage risk while riding.
At speed, counter-steer: press forward on the handgrip in the direction you want to turn or swerve.
A common car/motorcycle collision is an oncoming vehicle turning left across the rider's path; anticipate it at intersections.
DOT-compliant helmets are estimated to be about 37% effective in preventing fatal injuries to motorcycle riders.
A fully loaded tractor-trailer at 65 mph can need roughly 525+ feet to stop — far more than a passenger car; brake early.
On long downgrades use a lower gear and engine/auxiliary brakes to avoid brake fade from overheating.
Avoid hard braking in a turn; abrupt braking can cause the trailer to swing and jackknife.
A freight train traveling at typical speeds can take a mile or more to stop, even in an emergency.
Trains always have the right of way; never drive or walk around lowered crossing gates.
Blue signal protection: workers on, under, or between rolling equipment are protected by blue signals that only they may remove.
On station platforms, stay behind the tactile/yellow edge line until the train stops and doors open.
Ride in the same direction as traffic; bicyclists fare best when they act as, and are treated as, drivers of vehicles.
facts.source: League of American Bicyclists / Uniform Vehicle Code

facts.fact: Use a white front light and a red rear light/reflector when riding at night, as required by law in most states.
Wearing a bicycle helmet substantially reduces the risk of head and brain injury in a crash.
Avoid the 'door zone' — ride about a door's width away from parked cars to avoid being struck by an opening door.
Ride an e-scooter solo (one rider) and wear a helmet; doubling up degrades balance and braking.
Small scooter wheels are easily upset by potholes, curbs, and tram grooves — slow down and cross hazards at a square angle.
COLREGs Rule 5: every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper lookout by sight and hearing.
COLREGs Rule 6: every vessel shall proceed at a safe speed appropriate to conditions.
COLREGs Rule 7: use all available means to determine if risk of collision exists; if in doubt, assume it does.
Most boating-fatality drowning victims were not wearing a life jacket; wear a properly fitted PFD.
Cold-water immersion triggers an involuntary gasp and rapid loss of function; expect cold-shock and protect the airway.
facts.source: USCG / National Center for Cold Water Safety

facts.fact: Man overboard: assign a dedicated spotter to point continuously, throw flotation, and maneuver back to the person.
Caught in a rip current, don't fight it — swim parallel to shore to escape, then angle back in.
Cross at marked crosswalks or intersections, and make eye contact with drivers before stepping out.
The large majority of pedestrian fatalities occur after dark; increase caution and visibility at night.
Be visible at night: wear light-colored or reflective clothing and carry a light.
Watch for turning and reversing vehicles, especially quiet electric/hybrid cars that are hard to hear.
De-escalation uses time, distance, and cover to create options and slow a situation whenever feasible.
For behavioral-health crises, Crisis Intervention Team (CIT) approaches emphasize communication and connecting the person to care.
Use of force by police must be 'objectively reasonable' under the totality of the circumstances.
Active listening and rapport-building are core to crisis negotiation and gaining voluntary compliance.
For an active-shooter event, follow Run, Hide, Fight: evacuate if you can, hide and barricade if you cannot, and fight only as a last resort.
The Standard Response Protocol defines five actions: Lockout, Lockdown, Evacuate, Shelter, and Hold.
Teachers and school staff are mandated reporters and must report suspected child abuse or neglect (specifics vary by state).
For a tornado, move to the lowest interior room away from windows and protect your head and neck.
Account for every student during any emergency using current rosters; report anyone unaccounted for to responders.
facts.source: REMS TA Center (US Dept. of Education)

facts.fact: If approached by a stranger, children should keep distance, not accept rides or gifts, and go to a trusted adult right away.
facts.source: National Center for Missing & Exploited Children

facts.fact: High-quality CPR: push hard and fast in the center of the chest at 100–120 compressions per minute, at least 2 inches (5 cm) deep for adults, allowing full recoil.
Apply an AED as soon as it is available and follow the voice prompts; early defibrillation greatly improves survival.
Recognize stroke with BE-FAST: Balance, Eyes, Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services.
Anaphylaxis is treated first-line with intramuscular epinephrine; give it without delay and call EMS.
Control severe bleeding (Stop the Bleed): apply firm direct pressure, pack the wound, and use a tourniquet 'high and tight' if pressure fails.
For severe choking in a responsive adult, give abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) until the object clears or the person becomes unresponsive.
For suspected opioid overdose, give naloxone, call EMS, and support breathing; naloxone can be repeated if no response.
START triage sorts casualties by RPM — Respirations, Perfusion, and Mental status — into Immediate, Delayed, Minor, and Expectant.
Occupant fire response (RACE): Rescue anyone in danger, Alarm/activate, Confine the fire by closing doors, then Extinguish or Evacuate.
Operate a fire extinguisher with PASS: Pull the pin, Aim at the base, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.
Never use elevators during a fire; use the stairs.
Working smoke alarms cut the risk of dying in a reported home fire roughly in half.
Before opening a door during a fire, check it with the back of your hand; if hot, use another exit.
Use the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG) to find initial isolation and protective action distances for a released material.
Approach a hazardous release from uphill, upwind, and upstream, and stay clear of vapors and runoff.
Identify placards and UN/NA numbers from a safe distance to determine the hazard class before approaching.
For hypothermia, move the person out of the cold, remove wet clothing, insulate, and rewarm the core gently; handle them carefully.
Lightning safety: When thunder roars, go indoors. Follow the 30-30 rule and wait 30 minutes after the last thunder before resuming outdoor activity.
If lost outdoors, STOP: Stop, Think, Observe, Plan — staying put often aids rescue.
Turn Around, Don't Drown: six inches of moving water can knock you down and twelve inches can float many vehicles.
Report unattended or suspicious items and do not touch them — 'If You See Something, Say Something.'
Verify unusual payment or wire-transfer requests through a separate, trusted channel before acting (business email compromise defense).
For ransomware, isolate affected systems, preserve evidence, and report to CISA/FBI; paying ransom does not guarantee recovery.
Multi-factor authentication blocks the large majority of automated account-compromise attacks; enable it everywhere feasible.
Practice lateral reading: leave the page and check what independent, reputable sources say about a claim or website before trusting it.
Correlation does not imply causation; look for confounders and plausible mechanisms before concluding cause and effect.
In any emergency, ensure scene safety first — do not become a second victim.
The Incident Command System (ICS) provides a standard, scalable structure for managing emergencies and coordinating responders.
